examples: The fate of the three men is unknown.
She sat outside, waiting to find out her fate.
The court will decide our fate/fates.
Each of the managers suffered the same fate.
He warned about the scam so others could avoid a similar fate.
Inhabitants of war-torn areas have been abandoned to their fate.
From that moment our fate was sealed (= our future was decided).
She made a favourable impression on his parents.
The biography shows him in a favourable light.
She gained a highly favourable impression of the company.
The tunnel is a remarkable feat of engineering.
to accomplish/achieve/perform astonishing feats
That was no mean feat (= it was difficult to do).
feminist demands/ideas/theories
the feminist movement
Nineteenth-century feminists demanded equal education and employment opportunities for single women.
dietary fibre
Dried fruits are especially high in fibre.
a high-/low-fibre diet
He has been advised to increase his fibre intake.
bran fibre
a fierce dog
Two fierce eyes glared at them.
He suddenly looked fierce.
She spoke in a fierce whisper.
The more she struggled, the fiercer he became.
an air/oil filter
a coffee/water filter
filter paper for the coffee machine
He smokes cigarettes without filters.
All drinking water must be filtered.
Use a sun block that filters UVA effectively.
(figurative) My secretary is very good at filtering my calls (= making sure that calls that I do not want do not get through).
I got a parking fine for parking on double yellow lines.
Offenders will be liable to a heavy fine (= one that costs a lot of money).
Under the new law, motorists face fines of up to £1 000.
She has already paid over $2 000 in fines.
fine somebody (something) The magistrate fined him $400.
be fined (something) Any company found to be breaking these rules will be heavily fined.
be fined for (doing) something She was fined for speeding.
be fined something for (doing) something The company was fined £20 000 for breaching safety regulations.
He got fined £200 for parking illegally.
The police were issued with firearms.
to have an epileptic fit
He suffered from headaches and fainting fits.
Her fits are now controlled by drugs.
There are plans to make the race an annual fixture.
Saturday’s fixture against Liverpool
the season’s fixture list

flaw in something The report reveals fatal flaws in security at the airport.

seriously/fundamentally/fatally flawed
a flawed argument
the book’s flawed heroine
The study was methodologically flawed.
a series of fatally flawed judgements
She burst into tears and fled.
flee from somebody/something a camp for refugees fleeing from the war
flee to…/into… He fled to London after an argument with his family.
flee something He was caught trying to flee the country.
The driver had already fled the scene of the accident.
a fleet of destroyers
Nelson destroyed the Franco-Spanish fleet at the Battle of Trafalgar.
The trap had cut deeply into the rabbit's flesh.
Tigers are flesh-eating animals.
the smell of rotting flesh

An overdraft provides increased flexibility but at a higher cost.
Few businesses are flourishing in the present economic climate.
The arts began to flourish at that time.
There was a flourishing black market.
body fluids (= for example, blood)
The doctor told him to drink plenty of fluids.
cleaning fluid
old film footage of the moon landing
People see live footage of the war at home on their televisions.
The fact that I was a foreigner was a big disadvantage.
a move to forge new links between management and workers
Strategic alliances are being forged with major European companies.
She forged a new career in the music business.
They forged a lead in the first 30 minutes of the game.
This formula is used to calculate the area of a circle.
formulate something to formulate a policy/theory/plan/proposal
The compost is specially formulated for pot plants.
formulate something to do something This new kitchen cleaner is formulated to cut through grease and dirt.
They set forth at dawn.
Huge chimneys belched forth smoke and grime.
the forthcoming elections
a list of forthcoming books
the band’s forthcoming UK tour
The club's aim is to foster better relations within the community.
fragile china/glass/bones
Be careful not to drop it; it’s very fragile.
a franchise agreement/company
a catering/rail franchise

to operate a business under franchise
He spoke frankly about the ordeal.
They frankly admitted their responsibility.
It's very easy to get frustrated in this job.
His apathy just made her even more frustrated.
frustrated at/with something They felt frustrated at the lack of progress.
It's frustrating to have to wait so long.
The frustrating thing is, they probably won’t even be in when we get there.
in frustration Dave thumped the table in frustration.
frustration of (doing) something She couldn't stand the frustration of not being able to help.
sexual frustration
Bathrooms don't have to be purely functional.
The office was large and functional rather than welcoming.
Items such as musical instruments may be both functional and aesthetically pleasing.

Hundreds of people attended the funeral.
a funeral procession
a funeral march (= a sad piece of music suitable for funerals)

online/internet gambling
The police are trying to stop all illegal gambling.
The commission licenses and regulates casino gambling and horse racing in the state.
He ran up heavy gambling debts.
an illegal gambling den
to have a gambling addiction/habit
a social/family gathering
He was asked to speak at a gathering of religious leaders.
He met her gaze (= looked at her while she looked at him).
She dropped her gaze (= stopped looking).

He sat for hours just gazing into space.

genocide against somebody genocide against ethnic minorities

a cursory/brief/casual/furtive glance

He walked away without a backward glance.

She glanced at her watch.
He glanced around the room.
I glanced up quickly to see who had come in.
glimpse (of somebody/something) He caught a glimpse of her in the crowd.
I came up on deck to get my first glimpse of the island.
Fans crowded the street outside waiting for a glimpse of their hero.
glimpse at somebody/something I just got a glimpse at the baby, but she was very cute.
We congratulate you on this glorious victory.
This is a glorious chapter in our country's history.
The film looks back at Spain’s glorious past.
Olympic glory in the 100 metres
I do all the work and he gets all the glory.

He came home a rich man, covered in glory.

The government is stressing the link between aid and good governance in poor countries.

a debut album of uncommon grace and beauty
I grabbed him, but he slipped from my grasp.
She felt a firm grasp on her arm.
Don't let the situation escape from your grasp.
As soon as he relaxed his grasp on the rope, it was pulled out of his hands.
He grasped my hand and shook it warmly.
Kay grasped him by the wrist.

The consequences will be very grave if nothing is done.
We were in grave danger.
I fear you are making a very grave mistake.

in a grave The plague victims were buried in a mass grave.
on a grave There were flowers on the grave.
Newton’s law of gravity
New York’s grid of streets

They were able to share their common joys and griefs.
grief for somebody/something her grief for her dead husband
grief over something grief over the loss of friends
grief at something He could not hide his grief at her death.
She gave a broad grin.
a wry/sheepish grin
with a grin ‘No,’ he said with a grin.
They grinned with delight when they heard our news.
Stop grinning and tell me what happened!
grin at somebody She grinned amiably at us.
grin something He grinned a wide grin.
to grind coffee/corn
The animal has teeth that grind its food into a pulp.
Keep a tight grip on the rope.
to loosen/release/relax your grip
She tried to get a grip on the icy rock.
The climber slipped and lost her grip.
She struggled from his grip.
Try adjusting your grip on the racket.
grip something ‘Please don't go,’ he said, gripping her arm.
grip on to something She gripped on to the railing with both hands.
gross weight (= including the container or wrapping)
gross income/wage (= before taxes, etc. are taken away)
Investments showed a gross profit of 26 per cent.
The family have three children and a gross income of £50 000.
urban guerrillas (= those who fight in towns)
guerrilla war/warfare (= fought by guerrillas on one or both sides)
a guerrilla movement
guidance for teachers on how to use video in the classroom
under the guidance of somebody Activities all take place under the guidance of an experienced tutor.

a guilt complex (= an exaggerated sense of guilt)

I had a terrible pain in my guts after eating too many plums.
be hailed (as) something The conference was hailed as a great success.
The expedition was widely hailed as a success.

hail somebody/something as something Union leaders hailed the socialists’ victory as a huge step forward.
It's about halfway between London and Bristol.
He left halfway through the ceremony.
I'm afraid we're not even halfway there yet.

She walked towards him and then halted.
‘Halt!’ the Major ordered (= used as a command to soldiers).
halt somebody/something The police were halting traffic on the parade route.
The trial was halted after the first week.
a handful of rice
She scooped up handfuls of loose earth.

This horse needs firm handling.
Why is he being so polite, as if I need tactful handling?

handy hints/tips for removing stains
a handy-sized dictionary
sexual/racial harassment
workplace/street harassment (= that happens at work/in the street)
We supply computer hardware to businesses.
social/racial harmony
in harmony They lived together in perfect harmony.
in harmony with something the need to be in harmony with our environment

the harsh treatment of prisoners

harvest time
Farmers are extremely busy during the harvest.
The nuts are ready to harvest in September.
He looked at me with intense hatred.
There was fear and hatred in his voice.
hatred for somebody/something She felt nothing but hatred for her attacker.
hatred of somebody/something a profound hatred of war
hatred towards somebody feelings of hatred towards the bombers
racial hatred (= hatred for people from a different race)
The debate simply revived old hatreds.
A headless rider haunts the country lanes.
I’ll come back to haunt you!
a fire/safety hazard
hazard to somebody/something Growing levels of pollution represent a serious health hazard to the local population.
hazard of (doing) something Everybody is aware of the hazards of smoking.

heighten something The campaign is intended to heighten public awareness of the disease.

the social/political hierarchy
She's quite high up in the management hierarchy.
a high-profile campaign

hint at something What are you hinting at?
hint (that)… They hinted (that) there might be more job losses.
+ speech ‘I might know something about it,’ he hinted.
Many refugees have been forced to flee their homeland.
hook something + adv./prep. We hooked the trailer to the back of the car.
+ adv./prep. a dress that hooks at the back
hopeful (that…) I feel hopeful that we'll find a suitable house very soon.
hopeful about something He is not very hopeful about the outcome of the interview.
hopeful of doing something (British English) She is hopeful of returning to work soon.

A ship appeared on the horizon.
a large bull with curved horns
